iretrieval-augmented generation (RAG) enables LLMs to
access the most recent literature and generate verifiable
X
ranswers [4, 5, 6]. Equipping LLMs with research tools and
a
databases provides autonomy and transparency, capabilities
that general-purpose models cannot replicate. However,
deploying LLM applications in research ecosystems requires
overcoming additional challenges associated with handling
computational infrastructure, interface design, and
integration with existing tools.
We developed a Bioinformatics Retrieval Augmented
Digital assistant (BRAD) to streamline the deployment of
LLMs with biomedical research tools. BRAD provides a
chat interface to a LLM powered Agent capable of
information retrieval from user-provided documents, online
literature repositories, software pipelines, and public
databases. The agent-based design enables integration with
a wide range of computational tools and resources,
extending its capabilities beyond those of other RAG
systems [5, 6]. The modular architecture includes a flexible

The BRAD Python package provides a flexible platform for
automating bioinformatics workflow through the integration
of LLMs (fig. 1A). An Agent class – a virtual AI system
with memory and interaction capabilities – orchestrates
interactions between core and tool modules [7, 8, 9]. Core
modules manage the Agent’s memory and resource
utilization, such as LLM operations, while tool modules
connect the Agent to external resources like databases,
literature repositories, and software. To enhance usability,
the package can be deployed within Python environments,
as a command-line interface, or through a Graphical User
Interface (GUI). First, we overview of the Python package’s
architecture and then explain the tool modules and GUI
deployment.
2.1  Python Architecture
The Agent class serves as the user interface with BRAD. It
coordinates the Agent’s memory, manages the output
directory, and oversees the utilization of system resources,
including LLMs and external tools. The interfaces to these
tools are standardized, enabling an Agent to be configured
with different or new tools.
Tool modules extend the Agent’s functionality by
providing an interface to connect external databases,

following the RAG paradigm, where the LLM generated
reply is augmented by retrieval of relevant information from
external data sources. The modular design allows users to
configure their Agent with tools tailored to specific needs.
Moreover, the tool interface is minimal, ensuring that new
resources can be easily integrated into the framework.

(C)
Q: What are the coordinates of the genome?
RAG with gpt-4o
The coordinates of the genome can be likened to latitude 
and longitude on a globe, which are read by enhancers 
associated with determination genes. These coordinates help 
specify the development of structures like nerves and 
muscles at particular positions and times. In organisms like 
Drosophila, these coordinates are established by initial 
asymmetries in the developing egg, which are influenced by 
earlier asymmetries during ovary development. Sources: 
Weintraub, 1993
o1-preview
The coordinates of the genome refer to the specific 
positions or ranges on a DNA sequence where genes or 
other functional elements are located. These are typically 
defined by numerical positions on a chromosome, marking 
the start and end points of regions such as genes, exons, or 
regulatory elements. Genome coordinates enable precise 
mapping of genetic information for research, diagnostics, 
and therapeutic purposes.

BRAD is configured to integrate a broad range of LLMs.
Users can leverage locally hosted models, which can be
fine-tuned for domain-specific tasks, or connect to online
models such as NVIDIA’s NIM platform and OpenAI’s
offerings. BRAD is built using LangChain ensuring
compatibility and to facilitate the ability to integrate with
other standard frameworks [10].

3.1  Biomarker Identification Workflow.
Identifying novel biomarkers is a common research task
with broad applications [14]. We developed a Software tool
that interfaces an Agent with software external to BRAD,
including a biomarker selection pipeline. This module
follows a workflow similar to RAG, where the LLM is

BRAD is a prototype AI research assistant that integrates
large language models (LLMs) with information retrieval
from bioinformatics resources. Its modular architecture and
diverse data sources enhance the accuracy and transparency
of LLM-generated responses while offering flexibility to
incorporate additional tool modules. Distributed as a
Python package with a user-friendly GUI, BRAD is
designed to be both extensible and easy to deploy, making
it an adaptable research tool. BRAD is designed for both
extensibility and ease of deployment, making it an
adaptable and user-friendly research tool. This positions
BRAD as a valuable resource for researchers seeking to
integrate LLMs into their workflows and broader research
ecosystems.

The following metrics are evaluated:
• Total Time: the time required to respond to the users
• LLM Time: the time required for the LLM to perform
• Total Cost: the total cost of LLM API fees within a si
• LLM Cost: the cost of LLM API fees for the LLM to p
• Input Tokens: the number of tokens input for a single
• Output Tokens: the number of tokens output for a sin
• Total Tokens: the sum of input and output tokens for
We measured these seven metrics for user queries requiring B
question-answering (i.e., functioning purely as a chatbot), sea
and potentially loading data from a file), and interacting wit
documentation. See fig. 2 and table 1.

asreliableindicatorsforspecificconditionsorbiologicalstates. Belowisatypicalframeworktoexecutesuchapipeline:
Step 1: Load and Explore Data
1. InputData:
• RNAseqdatamatrix(e.g.,geneexpressioncounts).
• Associatedmetadata(e.g.,phenotypes,conditions,orclinicaloutcomes).
2. Preprocessing:
• Normalizerawcounts(e.g.,TPM,RPKM,orusingatoollikeDESeq2forvariancestabilization).
• Performexploratorydataanalysis(EDA)tocheckforbatcheffects,outliers,anddatadistribution.
Step 2: Preprocess and Normalize Data
• QualityControl:
– Filterlow-countgenes(e.g.,genesexpressedin<5%ofsamplesorwithcountsbelowathreshold).
– Removeunwantedvariation(batcheffectremovalusingtoolslikesvaorCombat).
• Normalization:
– Useappropriatenormalizationmethodssuchas:
∗ ‘TMM‘(TrimmedMeanofM-values)foredgeR.
∗ ‘VST‘(VarianceStabilizingTransformation)forDESeq2.
Step 3: Identify Differentially Expressed Genes (DEGs)
• PerformstatisticalteststofindDEGsbetweenconditionsorphenotypes:
– DESeq2oredgeRforcountdata.
– AdjustformultipletestingusingmethodslikeFDR(FalseDiscoveryRate)toidentifysignificantgenes(e.g.,FDR
<0.05).
Step 4: Dimensionality Reduction
• UsetechniqueslikePCA(PrincipalComponentAnalysis)ort-SNEtoreducedimensionalityandvisualizethemost
variablegenesacrossconditions.
Step 5: Feature Selection for Biomarker Discovery
• Applyfeatureselectiontechniquestopinpointbiomarkers:
– FilterMethods:
∗ SelecttopNgenesbasedonfoldchangeorp-value.
∗ Usevariancethresholdstofilterlessvariablegenes.
– WrapperMethods:
∗ RecursiveFeatureElimination(RFE)withclassifierslikeSVMorRandomForest.
